## 1. Introduction to PEFT

Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) contain tens to hundreds of billions of parameters. Full fine-tuning—updating every weight for each downstream task—is prohibitively expensive: it requires storing a complete copy of the model per task, demands multi-GPU memory for optimizer states and gradients, and risks catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) addresses this by modifying only a small fraction of the model's parameters (typically <1% of total) while keeping the vast majority frozen. This dramatically reduces memory footprint, training time, and storage costs, enabling practitioners to adapt a single base model to dozens of specialized tasks without duplicating the full architecture. Three landmark methods—Adapters, Prefix-Tuning, and LoRA—have defined the PEFT landscape and serve as the foundation for most subsequent work.

## 2. Paper Extraction Matrix

| Paper Title & Year | Core Method | Key Innovation | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **"Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP" (2019)** — Houlsby et al. | **Adapter layers**: Small bottleneck feed-forward modules inserted between the layers of a frozen pre-trained Transformer. Only adapter parameters are updated during fine-tuning. | First systematic demonstration that inserting <5% additional parameters per task can match full fine-tuning performance across 26 text classification tasks. Introduced the "bottleneck" design (down-project → non-linearity → up-project) with residual connections. | **Inference latency overhead**: Adapters are inserted *inside* the sequential forward pass of each Transformer layer, meaning every token must pass through the extra adapter computation. This adds 30–50% inference time compared to the base model, even after training is complete. |
| **"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation" (2021)** — Li & Liang | **Prefix-tuning**: Prepends a learnable continuous "prefix" (virtual token embeddings) to the keys and values of the multi-head attention in each Transformer layer. The base model remains frozen. | Introduced the concept of "soft prompts" that are not discrete words but continuous vectors in embedding space. Showed that steering attention via learned prefixes can outperform full fine-tuning on table-to-text and summarization tasks while only training 0.1% of parameters. | **Input length consumption**: The prefix occupies positions in the sequence, reducing the effective context window available for actual input tokens. For long-document tasks, this forces a trade-off between prefix expressiveness and usable context length. |
| **"LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models" (2021)** — Hu et al. | **Low-rank adaptation**: For each weight matrix \(W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\), learns a low-rank decomposition \(W + \Delta W = W + BA\) where \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}\) with rank \(r \ll \min(d,k)\). Only \(A\) and \(B\) are trained. | Proved that the *change* in weights during fine-tuning has low intrinsic rank. By decomposing updates into two smaller matrices, LoRA introduces zero additional inference latency (the trained matrices can be merged into the original weights) and reduces memory by up to 3× vs full fine-tuning. | **Rank selection is a hyperparameter**: The optimal rank \(r\) varies unpredictably across tasks and model sizes. A fixed rank may underfit (too low) or waste parameters (too high). There is no principled method to determine the correct rank a priori, requiring expensive grid search. |

## 1. Introduction to PEFT

Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) contain tens to hundreds of billions of parameters. Full fine-tuning—updating every weight for each downstream task—is prohibitively expensive: it requires storing a complete copy of the model per task, demands multi-GPU memory for optimizer states and gradients, and risks catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) addresses this by modifying only a small fraction of the model's parameters (typically <1% of total) while keeping the vast majority frozen. This dramatically reduces memory footprint, training time, and storage costs, enabling practitioners to adapt a single base model to dozens of specialized tasks without duplicating the full architecture. Three landmark methods—Adapters, Prefix-Tuning, and LoRA—have defined the PEFT landscape and serve as the foundation for most subsequent work.

Only adapter parameters are updated during fine-tuning. | First systematic demonstration that inserting <5% additional parameters per task can match full fine-tuning performance across 26 text classification tasks. Introduced the "bottleneck" design (down-project → non-linearity → up-project) with residual connections. | **Inference latency overhead**: Adapters are inserted *inside* the sequential forward pass of each Transformer layer, meaning every token must pass through the extra adapter computation. This adds 30–50% inference time compared to the base model, even after training is complete. |
| **"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation" (2021)** — Li & Liang | **Prefix-tuning**: Prepends a learnable continuous "prefix" (virtual token embeddings) to the keys and values of the multi-head attention in each Transformer layer. The base model remains frozen. | Introduced the concept of "soft prompts" that are not discrete words but continuous vectors in embedding space. Showed that steering attention via learned prefixes can outperform full fine-tuning on table-to-text and summarization tasks while only training 0.1% of parameters. | **Input length consumption**: The prefix occupies positions in the sequence, reducing the effective context window available for actual input tokens. For long-document tasks, this forces a trade-off between prefix expressiveness and usable context length. |
| **"LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models" (2021)** — Hu et al. | **Low-rank adaptation**: For each weight matrix \(W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\), learns a low-rank decomposition \(W + \Delta W = W + BA\) where \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}\) with rank \(r \ll \min(d,k)\). Only \(A\) and \(B\) are trained. | Proved that the *change* in weights during fine-tuning has low intrinsic rank. By decomposing updates into two smaller matrices, LoRA introduces zero additional inference latency (the trained matrices can be merged into the original weights) and reduces memory by up to 3× vs full fine-tuning. | **Rank selection is a hyperparameter**: The optimal rank \(r\) varies unpredictably across tasks and model sizes. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) addresses this by modifying only a small fraction of the model's parameters (typically <1% of total) while keeping the vast majority frozen. ## 1. Introduction to PEFT

Large Language Models (LLMs) now routinely exceed 100 billion parameters. Full fine-tuning—updating every weight for each downstream task—imposes prohibitive costs: it requires storing a complete copy of the model per task, demands multi-GPU memory for optimizer states and gradients, and risks catastrophic forgetting of general pre-trained knowledge. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) solves this by modifying only a tiny fraction of parameters (typically <1%) while keeping the vast majority frozen. This dramatically reduces memory footprint, training time, and storage overhead, enabling practitioners to adapt a single base model to dozens of specialized tasks without duplicating the full architecture. Three landmark methods—Adapters, Prefix-Tuning, and LoRA—have defined the PEFT landscape and serve as the foundation for most subsequent research.

## 2. Paper Extraction Matrix

| Paper Title & Year | Core Method | Key Innovation | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **"Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP" (2019)** — Houlsby et al. | **Adapter layers**: Small bottleneck feed-forward modules inserted between the layers of a frozen pre-trained Transformer. Only adapter parameters are updated during fine-tuning. | First systematic demonstration that inserting a small number of additional parameters per task (approximately 3–5% of the base model) can match full fine-tuning performance across a wide range of text classification tasks. Introduced the "bottleneck" design (down-project → non-linearity → up-project) with residual connections. | **Inference latency overhead**: Adapters are inserted *inside* the sequential forward pass of each Transformer layer, meaning every token must pass through the extra adapter computation. This adds a small but non-zero computational cost at inference time (approximately 3–5% additional FLOPs per adapter), and the adapters cannot be merged into the base weights, so the overhead persists after training. |
| **"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation" (2021)** — Li & Liang | **Prefix-tuning**: Prepends a learnable continuous "prefix" (virtual token embeddings) to the keys and values of the multi-head attention in each Transformer layer. The base model remains frozen. | Introduced the concept of "soft prompts" that are not discrete words but continuous vectors in embedding space. Showed that steering attention via learned prefixes can outperform full fine-tuning on table-to-text and summarization tasks while only training 0.1% of parameters. | **Input length consumption**: The prefix occupies positions in the sequence, reducing the effective context window available for actual input tokens. For long-document tasks, this forces a trade-off between prefix expressiveness and usable context length. |
| **"LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models" (2021)** — Hu et al. | **Low-rank adaptation**: For each weight matrix \(W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\), learns a low-rank decomposition \(W + \Delta W = W + BA\) where \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}\) with rank \(r \ll \min(d,k)\). Only \(A\) and \(B\) are trained. | Proved that the *change* in weights during fine-tuning has low intrinsic rank. By decomposing updates into two smaller matrices, LoRA introduces zero additional inference latency (the trained matrices can be merged into the original weights) and reduces memory by up to 3× vs full fine-tuning. | **Rank selection is a hyperparameter**: The optimal rank \(r\) varies unpredictably across tasks and model sizes. A fixed rank may underfit (too low) or waste parameters (too high). There is no principled method to determine the correct rank a priori, requiring expensive grid search. |

## 3. Synthesized Research Gap & Future Direction

**Synthesized Research Gap**: Across all three methods, a common limitation emerges: **PEFT methods impose a static architectural or computational cost that is determined before training and cannot adapt to the difficulty of the task**. Adapters add fixed per-layer overhead that persists at inference; Prefix-Tuning consumes a fixed number of input positions regardless of task complexity; and LoRA requires a predetermined rank that may be suboptimal. None of these methods can dynamically allocate more capacity to harder layers or tasks while using fewer parameters for easier ones. This static allocation means that practitioners must either over-provision capacity (wasting parameters) or under-provision (sacrificing performance), with no principled way to know the optimal configuration in advance.

**Concrete Future Direction**: **Develop a dynamic, task-adaptive PEFT framework that automatically determines the required capacity per layer during training.** For example, a method could learn a gating mechanism that allocates adapter modules, prefix lengths, or LoRA ranks on a per-layer basis, expanding capacity only where the gradient signal indicates high task-specific deviation from the pre-trained weights. Such an approach would combine the zero-inference-overhead benefit of LoRA (by merging trained weights) with the architectural flexibility to allocate more parameters to layers that need them, while automatically pruning redundant capacity. This would eliminate the manual hyperparameter search for rank, prefix length, or adapter placement that currently plagues all three methods.

## 4. References

- Houlsby, N., Giurgiu, A., Jastrzebski, S., Morrone, B., de Laroussilhe, Q., Gesmundo, A., Attariyan, M., & Gelly, S. (2019). Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP. *arXiv:1902.00751*. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.00751

- Li, X. L., & Liang, P. (2021). Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation. *arXiv:2101.00190*. https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.00190

- Hu, E. J., Shen, Y., Wallis, P., Allen-Zhu, Z., Li, Y., Wang, S., Wang, L., & Chen, W. (2021). LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. *arXiv:2106.09685*. https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685
